Endoscopic Diagnosis and Management of Peptic Ulcer Bleeding: ESGE Guideline Update 2026
  • Source: Endoscopy, Vol. 58, 2026 (Published online: 2026-05-13)
  • Topic: Nonvariceal Upper GI Hemorrhage - Peptic Ulcer Bleeding Management
  • Author(s): Gralnek IM, Morris J, Laursen SB, et al. (20 authors total)
1. Pre-Endoscopy Management
1.1 Initial Patient Evaluation and Hemodynamic Resuscitation
  • Immediate hemodynamic status assessment --> required for all acute UGIH patients
  • Prompt intravascular volume replacement using crystalloid fluids --> if hemodynamic instability exists
  • Hemodynamic instability definitions: SBP <100 mmHg alone, SBP <90 mmHg alone, or SBP <100 mmHg + pulse >100 bpm
  • Hemodynamic instability affects 1 in 4 patients presenting with acute nonvariceal UGIH
  • Hemodynamic instability --> ↑ in-hospital mortality (OR 4.79, 95%CI 2.62–8.97)
  • Hemodynamic instability --> ↑ in-hospital rebleeding (OR 4.95, 95%CI 1.70–14.44)
  • Address underlying patient comorbidities before upper GI endoscopy
1.2 Red Blood Cell (RBC) Transfusion Strategy
  • Restrictive transfusion strategy recommended for hemodynamically stable patients without cardiovascular disease history
  • Hb threshold <7 g/dL prompts RBC transfusion
  • Post-transfusion target Hb: 7–9 g/dL
  • Liberal transfusion strategy recommended for hemodynamically stable patients with acute or chronic cardiovascular disease history
  • Hb threshold ≤8 g/dL prompts RBC transfusion
  • Post-transfusion target Hb: ≥10 g/dL
  • Kola et al. RCT (n=224): 45-day mortality similar between restrictive vs liberal groups (10/112 vs. 12/112; P=0.65)
  • Teutsch et al. meta-analysis (7 RCTs of UGIH): restrictive strategy ↓ RBC units transfused
  • Lower Hb thresholds (>8 g/dL) associated with more adverse outcomes
1.3 Patient Risk Stratification
  • Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) recommended for pre-endoscopy risk stratification
  • GBS ≤1 --> patients safely managed as outpatients with outpatient endoscopy
  • Low-risk patients must have: prompt GI/endoscopy follow-up, ability to return to ED if needed, understanding of clinical plan
  • No risk stratification tool should replace clinician judgment
1.3.1 Video Capsule Endoscopy and Telemetric Blood-Sensing Capsules
  • ESGE does NOT recommend routine use of video capsule endoscopy or telemetric blood-sensing capsules in suspected UGIH
  • Capsule-based strategies show signals of benefit primarily in patient triage
  • Video capsule endoscopy in ED: ↑ same-day discharge, ↓ need for urgent inpatient upper GI endoscopy
  • No evidence of rebleeding or mortality difference at 7–30 days in hemodynamically stable patients
  • Telemetric blood-sensing capsules: high technical success and diagnostic accuracy for detecting upper GI blood
  • Negative capsule tests --> absence of rebleeding, may allow downgrading of urgent endoscopy need
  • Meta-analyses: capsule triage pathways ↓ hospital admissions and early upper GI endoscopy in suspected UGIH
  • AI-assisted wireless capsule endoscopy: enhances speed/consistency but no patient-level outcome data yet
  • Limitations: small datasets, observational studies, heterogeneous inclusion criteria and endpoints
1.4 Prokinetic Medications
  • Intravenous erythromycin recommended pre-endoscopy in selected patients with clinically severe/ongoing active UGIH
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • Intravenous metoclopramide suggested as alternative if erythromycin unavailable (conditional recommendation, low quality evidence)
  • Meta-analysis of 4 RCTs comparing IV metoclopramide vs. placebo:
  • Metoclopramide → improved mucosal exposure and endoscopic visualization (Mean difference 0.37, 95%CI 0.12–0.62; P=0.004)
  • No significant differences in units of blood transfused or hospital length of stay
  • Possible trend toward reduced need for second-look endoscopy (RR 0.60, 95%CI 0.32–1.11; P=0.10)
  • Waseem et al. meta-analysis (published after initial search): similar results
  • Metoclopramide advantages: widespread availability, feasible, low cost
  • Metoclopramide risks: anaphylaxis, serotonin syndrome, extrapyramidal reaction, cardiac arrhythmia
  • Only one RCT among included studies provided adverse events data --> no adverse events reported
1.4.1 Criteria for Selected Patients Receiving IV Erythromycin
  • Acute UGIH presentation (fresh blood hematemesis, coffee ground emesis, melena)
  • Persisting hematemesis
  • Clinical suspicion for residual blood/clots in upper GI tract
  • No known macrolide allergy
  • No known QT interval prolongation
  • No concomitant use of medication with possible interaction with macrolide antibiotics
1.5 Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs)
  • Pre-endoscopy high dose IV PPI suggested in acute UGIH patients
  • Conditional recommendation, low quality evidence
  • Pre-endoscopy PPI should NOT delay early endoscopy
  • Cochrane meta-analysis update (2022): pre-endoscopy PPI may:
  • ↓ need for endoscopic hemostasis at index endoscopy (OR 0.68, 95%CI 0.50–0.93)
  • May reduce rebleeding (OR 0.81, 95%CI 0.62–1.06)
  • Pre-endoscopy PPI does NOT reduce:
  • Mortality (OR 1.14, 95%CI 0.76–1.70)
  • Need for surgery (OR 0.91, 95%CI 0.65–1.26)
  • Proportion with high risk endoscopic stigmata at index endoscopy (OR 0.80, 95%CI 0.52–1.21)
  • Italian multicenter retrospective cohort (n=2566): no difference in high risk stigmata prevalence between pre-endoscopy PPI vs. no PPI (51.8% vs. 53.4%; P=0.58)
  • Multivariate analysis: PPI not independent predictor of high risk stigmata prevalence (OR 1.16, 95%CI 0.82–1.64; P=0.40)
1.6 Endotracheal Intubation
  • Routine prophylactic intubation NOT recommended for airway protection before upper GI endoscopy in acute UGIH
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • Prophylactic intubation recommended only in selected patients:
  • Ongoing active hematemesis
  • Agitation
  • Encephalopathy with inability to adequately control airway
  • Recent observational retrospective data confirm: ↑ pulmonary adverse events (aspiration, pneumonia), longer hospital stays without consistent mortality benefit
  • New studies remain at high risk of confounding
2. Endoscopic Management
2.1 Timing of Upper GI Endoscopy
  • Timing definitions (relative to patient presentation):
  • Emergent: ≤6 hours
  • Urgent: ≤12 hours
  • Early: ≤24 hours
  • Delayed: >24 hours
  • ESGE does NOT recommend emergent (≤6h) or urgent (≤12h) upper GI endoscopy unless patient remains hemodynamically unstable despite adequate resuscitation
  • Strong recommendation, moderate quality evidence
  • Early (≤24 hours) upper GI endoscopy after hemodynamic resuscitation recommended
  • Strong recommendation, low quality evidence
  • Early endoscopy associated with: ↑ likelihood of endoscopic hemostasis use, modest ↓ in hospital length of stay
  • Early endoscopy shows NO reduction in mortality or rebleeding vs. delayed endoscopy (mortality 3%–6%, rebleeding 7%–11%)
  • Urgent/emergent endoscopy NOT shown to improve outcomes vs. early endoscopy
  • Lau et al. RCT: no improvement in 30-day mortality, rebleeding, transfusion requirements, or hospital stay with endoscopy within 6 hours vs. 6–24 hours
  • Findings likely influenced by confounding by indication and incomplete hemodynamic resuscitation
2.2 Endoscopic Diagnosis - Forrest Classification
  • Forrest (F) classification must be used in all peptic ulcer hemorrhage patients to differentiate low and high risk endoscopic stigmata
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • High-risk lesions requiring endoscopic hemostasis:
  • FIa: Spurting hemorrhage
  • FIb: Oozing hemorrhage
  • FIIa: Nonbleeding visible vessel
  • Low-risk lesions NOT requiring endoscopic hemostasis:
  • FIIc: Flat pigmented spot
  • FIII: Clean base ulcer
2.2.1 Adherent Clot (Forrest IIb) Management
  • ESGE suggests patients with FIIb undergo endoscopic therapy with clot removal + subsequent endoscopic hemostasis if indicated
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • Prerequisite: endoscopist has technical competence to safely remove clot and manage potential conversion to higher risk bleeding lesion
  • Beran et al. meta-analysis (11 studies, 9 RCTs; n=833):
  • Endoscopic therapy vs. conservative treatment:
  • Overall rebleeding: OR 0.41, 95%CI 0.22–0.79; P=0.007
  • 30-day rebleeding: OR 0.43, 95%CI 0.21–0.89; P=0.002
  • Overall mortality: OR 0.47, 95%CI 0.23–0.95; P=0.04
  • Need for surgery: OR 0.44, 95%CI 0.21–0.95; P=0.04
  • Hospital stay: mean shortening by 3.17 days (95%CI −4.14 to −2.19; P<0.001)
  • Subgroup analysis restricted to 9 RCTs:
  • Mortality difference NOT significant (OR 0.78, 95%CI 0.24–2.52; P=0.68)
  • Trend toward reduced overall rebleeding (7.2% vs. 18.5%; OR 0.42, 95%CI 0.17–1.05; P=0.06)
  • Statistically significant reduction in need for surgery (OR 0.28, 95%CI 0.08–0.96; P=0.04)
  • Tassone et al. meta-analysis (7 RCTs, n=268):
  • Recurrent bleeding: RR 0.40, 95%CI 0.16–0.95
  • No significant differences in mortality (RR 0.90, 95%CI 0.23–3.59) or need for surgery (RR 0.48, 95%CI 0.10–2.28)
  • Clot removal often exposes underlying higher risk stigma (FIa, FIb, or FIIa)
  • Practical consideration: preinjection of diluted epinephrine around/under clot before removal to mitigate brisk bleeding risk
  • Clinical benefit closely linked to: endoscopist competence in clot removal, recognition of underlying stigmata, management of spurting hemorrhage
2.2.2 Doppler Endoscopic Probe (DEP)
  • ESGE could not reach consensus for or against routine use of DEP in treatment decisions for high risk endoscopic stigmata
  • No recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • DEP use concerns: generalizability in clinical practice, cost-effectiveness data lacking, equipment/training costs
  • Persistent positive DEP signal after endoscopic hemostasis: 100% vs. 11% rebleeding rate (P=0.003)
  • Nielsen et al. RCT (n=62, FIa–FIIb peptic ulcer bleeding):
  • Second-look endoscopy with DEP <24 hours vs. standard treatment
  • 91% of patients had positive Doppler signal at follow-up endoscopy, all retreated
  • No significant difference in rebleeding (3% vs. 13%; P=0.20), transfusion, hospital stay, or mortality
  • Bhurwal et al. meta-analysis: DEP ↓ rebleeding, mortality, surgical intervention vs. visual observation
  • Persistent Doppler signal despite endoscopic therapy: rebleeding 48.5% (95%CI 29.5%–67.9%)
  • Chapelle et al. meta-analysis: DEP during upper GI endoscopy significantly ↓ overall rebleeding (OR 0.27, 95%CI 0.14–0.54), bleeding-related mortality, need for surgery
  • Arterial blood flow underneath stigmata NOT obliterated in 25%–30% of patients after visually-guided treatment --> leads to rebleeding
2.3 Endoscopic Hemostasis
2.3.1 Active Bleeding (Forrest Ia/Ib)
  • Combination therapy recommended: dilute epinephrine injection + second modality
  • Second modality: contact thermal OR mechanical (TTS clip, OTS clip)
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • OTS clips as monotherapy suggested as alternative to combination therapy for FIa, FIb
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • OTS clips show: lower risk of further bleeding vs. standard endoscopic hemostatic therapy
  • Hemostatic forceps with soft coagulation suggested as monotherapy for FIa, FIb
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
2.3.2 Nonbleeding Visible Vessel (Forrest IIa)
  • Contact or noncontact thermal therapy, mechanical therapy (TTS clips, OTS clips), or injection of sclerosing agent recommended
  • Each as monotherapy OR in combination with epinephrine injection
  • Strong recommendation, moderate quality evidence
  • OTS clips suggested as alternative monotherapy for FIIa
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • Hemostatic forceps with soft coagulation suggested as monotherapy for FIIa
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
2.3.3 Epinephrine Monotherapy
  • DO NOT use epinephrine injection as endoscopic monotherapy
  • If used, must be combined with a second endoscopic hemostasis modality
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
2.3.4 Topical Hemostatic Agents
  • NOT recommended as monotherapy in first-line treatment of high risk endoscopic stigmata peptic ulcer bleeding
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • Topical agents retain role as: rescue therapy in refractory ulcer bleeding, temporizing measure for subsequent definitive therapy
  • Recent RCT (n=348 nonvariceal UGIH, 317 with high risk FIa–FIIb peptic ulcer bleeding):
  • Adjuvant topical hemostatic agent after successful initial hemostasis vs. no adjuvant
  • At 72 hours: 5/167 (3.0%) vs. 18/150 (12.0%); P=0.004
  • At 30 days: 12/167 (7.2%) vs. 29/150 (19.3%); P=0.004
  • No adverse events reported with adjuvant topical hemostatic agent
2.3.5 Persistent/Recurrent Bleeding Management
  • Persistent bleeding definition: ongoing active bleeding refractory to standard hemostasis modalities
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • Recurrent bleeding definition: bleeding following initial successful endoscopic hemostasis
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
2.3.5.1 Persistent Bleeding Refractory to Standard Modalities
  • Suggest use of topical hemostatic agent or OTS clips
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
2.3.5.2 Persistent Bleeding Refractory to All Modalities (Including Topical Agents and OTS Clips)
  • Transcatheter angiographic embolization (TAE) recommended
  • Strong recommendation, moderate quality evidence
  • Surgery indicated when TAE not locally available or after unsuccessful TAE
2.3.5.3 Recurrent Peptic Ulcer Bleeding - Second Attempt Endoscopy
  • Consider OTS clip for recurrent bleeding after initial endoscopic hemostasis
  • Strong recommendation, moderate quality evidence
  • If second attempt at endoscopic hemostasis also unsuccessful: TAE recommended
  • Surgery indicated when TAE locally unavailable or after unsuccessful TAE
2.3.6 Evidence for OTS Clips vs. Standard Therapy
  • Multiple recent RCTs and meta-analyses evaluating cap-mounted OTS clips as first-line monotherapy in high risk nonvariceal UGIH
  • OTS clips demonstrate: significant reduction in composite outcome of further bleeding at 30 days (driven primarily by lower recurrent bleeding rates)
  • No clear advantage for: immediate hemostasis, mortality, or need for surgery
  • Overall certainty of evidence: very low to low due to methodological limitations (lack of blinding, heterogeneity in patient selection, ulcer characteristics, comparator therapies)
  • Variable use of epinephrine injection and differences in operator expertise across studies
  • Recent cost-effectiveness analyses: OTS clips cost-effective in high risk peptic ulcer bleeding (especially when used upfront vs. rescue therapy)
  • Available RCT evidence exclusively evaluates Ovesco Endoscopy (Tübingen, Germany) OTS clip system
2.3.7 Hemostatic Forceps
  • Very low quality evidence shows:
  • High rates of initial hemostasis
  • Reduced rebleeding
  • Shorter procedure times vs. conventional modalities
2.3.8 Comparative Outcomes: OTS Clip vs. TAE for Refractory Bleeding
  • Kuellmer et al. multicenter retrospective study:
  • Clinical success comparable: OTS clip 74.2% vs. TAE 59.7%; P=0.09
  • Mean ICU stay: OTS clip 4.7 days vs. TAE 8.0 days; P=0.002
  • Serious adverse events: OTS clip 1.5% vs. TAE 12.9%; P=0.04
  • In-hospital mortality: OTS clip 9.1% vs. TAE 22.6%; OR 2.92 (95%CI 1.04–8.16); P=0.05
3. Postendoscopy Management
3.1 Prophylactic Transcatheter Angiographic Embolization (TAE)
  • ESGE suggests prophylactic TAE be considered in selected high risk cases of peptic ulcer bleeding
  • Conditional recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • High-risk criteria for prophylactic TAE:
  • Hemodynamic instability at presentation
  • Posterior duodenal wall ulcer location
  • Large ulcer size (>2 cm)
  • When durable endoscopic hemostasis considered uncertain
  • Evidence from 4 meta-analyses + 3 retrospective cohort studies (2020–2025) + 2 previously published RCTs
  • Two RCTs suggest lower rebleeding rates with prophylactic TAE (not always statistically significant)
  • Post-hoc analysis: prophylactic TAE significantly ↓ recurrent bleeding only in patients with ulcers ≥15mm (2 [4.5%] vs. 12 [23.1%]; P=0.03)
  • Observational data and meta-analyses report: significant reductions in rebleeding and need for surgery/reintervention
  • Mortality does NOT differ consistently between groups
  • Hospital length of stay varies minimally
3.2 Antisecretory Therapy - Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs)
  • High dose PPI therapy recommended for patients who have undergone endoscopic hemostasis
  • Also recommended for FIIb ulcer stigmata (adherent clot) not treated endoscopically
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
3.2.1 PPI Dosing Regimens
  • Standard regimen: IV bolus 80 mg + continuous infusion 8 mg/hour for 72 hours post-endoscopy
  • Alternative regimens:
  • IV bolus twice daily (high dose)
  • Oral formulation twice daily
3.2.2 Recent Evidence on PPI Regimens
  • Two meta-analyses, one combined RCT/cohort study, one prospective observational cohort, three retrospective single-center studies (since 2021)
  • No significant differences in patient outcomes when comparing:
  • Oral vs. IV PPI
  • High dose vs. low dose PPI
  • Two retrospective single-center studies reported conflicting results for rebleeding rates (both at high risk of bias)
3.2.3 Potassium-Competitive Acid Blockers (P-CABs)
  • ESGE could not reach consensus for or against routine use of P-CABs after endoscopic hemostasis
  • No recommendation, very low quality evidence
  • Two recent RCTs demonstrate comparable efficacy between vonoprazan and PPI in preventing recurrent bleeding:
  • Pilot RCT (n=44): no significant difference at 3, 7, or 30 days (18.2% vs. 11.1%; P>0.99)
  • Thai multicenter non-inferiority RCT (n=194): no difference in 30-day rebleeding (7.1% vs. 10.4%; P<0.001 for noninferiority)
3.3 Recurrent Bleeding Management
  • Recurrent bleeding defined: bleeding following initial successful endoscopic hemostasis
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
3.3.1 Clinical Evidence of Recurrent Bleeding
  • Recurrent hematemesis or bloody nasogastric aspirate after first endoscopy
  • Recurrent tachycardia or hypotension after achieving hemodynamic stability
  • Melena and/or hematochezia following normalization of stool color
  • Reduction in Hb ≥2 g/dL after stable Hb value attained
3.3.2 Management Approach
  • Repeat upper GI endoscopy with hemostasis if indicated recommended
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • Consider OTS clip for recurrent peptic ulcer bleeding
  • Strong recommendation, moderate quality evidence
  • If second attempt at endoscopic hemostasis unsuccessful: TAE recommended
  • Surgery indicated when TAE locally unavailable or after unsuccessful TAE
3.4 Restarting Anticoagulation
  • Resume anticoagulation as soon as clinically indicated based on thromboembolic risk
  • Strong recommendation, low quality evidence
  • Recent UK audit (n=5141): 30.6% of acute UGIH patients on anticoagulant at bleeding episode (↑ from 13% in 2007 audit)
  • Decision must balance: risk of recurrent bleeding vs. risk of thromboembolic events/sequelae (including death)
  • Patients restarted on anticoagulation after peptic ulcer bleed:
  • Median restart: 15 days
  • Lower thrombotic/death risk at 1 year (HR 0.14, 95%CI 0.05–0.43)
  • No significantly ↑ recurrent bleeding risk (HR 1.42, 95%CI 0.10–19.8)
  • Precise timing remains undefined; highest thrombotic risk patients should restart ASAP
  • Consider low molecular weight heparin as bridge to oral anticoagulation
  • Early consultation with cardiologist and/or hematologist desirable
  • VKAs require longer time to adequate anticoagulation (up to 5 days) vs. DOACs (hours)
  • Use validated scores: CHA₂DS₂-VASc for thrombotic risk, HAS-BLED for bleeding risk
3.5 Helicobacter pylori Management
  • Test for H. pylori at index endoscopy (acute setting)
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • If H. pylori positive: initiate appropriate antibiotic therapy
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • If H. pylori negative at first endoscopy: repeat testing required to confirm true negativity
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
  • PPIs should be withheld for at least 2 weeks before repeat H. pylori testing to avoid false-negative results
  • Document successful H. pylori eradication
  • Strong recommendation, high quality evidence
3.6 Iron Therapy
  • ESGE suggests iron therapy initiated prior to hospital discharge in patients with peptic ulcer bleeding and iron deficiency and/or anemia
  • Conditional recommendation, low quality evidence
3.6.1 Evidence for Iron Therapy
  • Chang et al. RCT (89% peptic ulcer bleeding):
  • Oral ferrous fumarate 600 mg daily vs. no iron treatment
  • Normalized Hb or ↑ beta-Hb ≥2 g/dL at 6 weeks: 72.7% vs. 45.9%; adjusted RR 2.98; P=0.004
  • 53% of untreated patients had ongoing iron deficiency (ferritin <30 µg/L and transferrin saturation <16%; P<0.05)
  • Bager et al. RCT (70% peptic ulcer bleeding):
  • Single-dose IV iron 1000 mg vs. oral iron 200 mg daily for 3 months vs. placebo
  • At 3 months: Hb levels 13.9 g/dL (IV), 13.5 g/dL (oral), 11.5 g/dL (placebo); P<0.01
  • Full iron stores at 3 months (ferritin >100 µg/L): IV 41%, oral 24%, placebo 10%; P=0.11
  • Patients with normalized Hb reported higher quality of life at 6 months follow-up
  • Ferrer-Barceló et al. RCT:
  • IV iron vs. oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg twice daily for 6 weeks
  • Normalized Hb: 100% vs. 61%; P<0.001
  • Better subjective state of health: P=0.002
  • Meta-analysis (2 RCTs): oral iron therapy → significantly higher Hb from week 4 onwards
  • Mean Hb difference: 0.78, 95%CI 0.27–1.28; P=0.002
3.7 Restarting Oral Nutrition
  • ESGE suggests early oral nutrition within 24 hours following endoscopic hemostasis in patients with peptic ulcer bleeding in whom durable hemostasis achieved
  • Conditional recommendation, low quality evidence
3.7.1 Evidence for Early Oral Nutrition
  • Meta-analysis of 10 RCTs (n=1051 UGIH patients) comparing early vs. delayed oral nutrition after endoscopic hemostasis
  • Subgroup analysis (peptic ulcer bleeding patients, n=560):
  • Early rebleeding (within 7 days): RR 0.95, 95%CI 0.54–1.68
  • Late rebleeding (within 30 days): RR 1.14, 95%CI 0.16–7.98
  • Early mortality: RR 0.98, 95%CI 0.85–1.14
  • Late mortality: RR 0.51, 95%CI 0.03–7.83
  • Hospital length of stay: mean difference −1.34 days, 95%CI −5.01 to 2.33
  • No significant differences in any outcome between early vs. delayed nutrition groups
4. Algorithm Summary - Peptic Ulcer Bleeding Management Flowchart
4.1 Initial Assessment
  • Acute UGIH suspected --> Assess hemodynamic status
  • Hemodynamically stable + GBS ≤1 --> Consider outpatient management with outpatient endoscopy
  • Hemodynamically unstable --> Resuscitation (IV crystalloid fluids, restrictive RBC transfusion policy)
4.2 Endoscopy Timing
  • Early upper GI endoscopy (≤24 hours) after hemodynamic resuscitation
  • NOT emergent/urgent unless patient remains hemodynamically unstable despite adequate resuscitation
4.3 Forrest Classification and Treatment
  • Forrest Ia/Ib (Active bleeding - spurting/oozing):
  • Combination therapy: dilute epinephrine + second modality (contact thermal OR mechanical [TTS clip, OTS clip])
  • Alternative: OTS clip monotherapy
  • Alternative: hemostatic forceps (soft coagulation)
  • Forrest IIa (Nonbleeding visible vessel):
  • Monotherapy ± dilute epinephrine: contact/noncontact thermal, mechanical (TTS clips, OTS clips), or sclerosant injection
  • Alternative: hemostatic forceps (soft coagulation)
  • Forrest IIb (Adherent clot):
  • Clot removal + treat underlying endoscopic stigmata
  • Preinject dilute epinephrine before clot removal
  • Requires endoscopist technical competence
  • Forrest IIc/III (Flat pigmented spot/Clean base - low risk):
  • No endoscopic therapy needed
  • Proceed to postendoscopy medical management
  • Consider early hospital discharge if low risk
4.4 Persistent/Recurrent Bleeding
  • Repeat endoscopy:
  • Consider OTS clip if not initially used
  • Consider topical hemostatic agent as rescue therapy
  • If endoscopy unsuccessful:
  • Transcatheter angiographic embolization (TAE)
  • Surgery (if TAE unavailable/unsuccessful)
4.5 Postendoscopy Management
  • High dose PPI (IV bolus + continuous infusion OR minimum twice-daily IV bolus for 72 hours OR oral)
  • May start clear liquids within 24 hours after endoscopy
  • Test for H. pylori at index endoscopy, treat if positive
  • If H. pylori negative at first endoscopy: repeat testing within 4 weeks to confirm true negative
  • Consider initiating iron therapy prior to hospital discharge for iron deficiency and/or anemia
  • Anticoagulant/antiplatelet management if applicable
5. Key Recommendations Summary Table
5.1 Pre-Endoscopy Management
Recommendation Strength
Immediate hemodynamic assessment + crystalloid fluids if unstable Strong/Moderate
Restrictive RBC transfusion (Hb <7 g/dL, target 7–9 g/dL) for stable patients without CV disease Strong/Moderate
Liberal RBC transfusion (Hb ≤8 g/dL, target ≥10 g/dL) for stable patients with CV disease Strong/Low
GBS ≤1 = outpatient management safe Strong/Moderate
NO routine video capsule endoscopy or telemetric blood-sensing capsules Strong/Very Low
IV erythromycin pre-endoscopy in selected severe/active UGIH patients Strong/High
IV metoclopramide if erythromycin unavailable (selected patients) Conditional/Low
Pre-endoscopy high dose IV PPI; should NOT delay early endoscopy Conditional/Low
5.2 Endoscopic Management
Recommendation Strength
Early (≤24h) upper GI endoscopy after hemodynamic resuscitation Strong/Low
NO emergent/urgent endoscopy unless hemodynamically unstable despite resuscitation Strong/Moderate
Use Forrest classification for all peptic ulcer hemorrhage patients Strong/High
FIa, FIb, FIIa receive endoscopic hemostasis (high risk) Strong/High
FIIc, FIII: NO endoscopic hemostasis (low risk) Strong/Moderate
FIIb: Consider clot removal + endoscopic therapy if endoscopist competent Conditional/Very Low
FIa/Ib: Combination therapy (epinephrine + thermal OR mechanical) Strong/High
FIa/Ib: OTS clip monotherapy as alternative first-line therapy Conditional/Very Low
FIIa: Contact/noncontact thermal, mechanical (TTS/OTS clips), or sclerosant ± epinephrine Strong/Moderate
FIIa: OTS clips may be used as alternative monotherapy Conditional/Very Low
Hemostatic forceps with soft coagulation may be used as monotherapy (FIa, FIb, FIIa) Conditional/Very Low
DO NOT use epinephrine monotherapy Strong/High
Topical hemostatic agents NOT recommended as first-line monotherapy Conditional/Very Low
Persistent bleeding: consider topical agent or OTS clips Conditional/Very Low
All modalities failed: TAE recommended; surgery if TAE unavailable/unsuccessful Strong/Moderate
5.3 Postendoscopy Management
Recommendation Strength
High dose PPI (IV bolus + continuous infusion 80mg+8mg/h for 72h OR IV bid OR oral bid) Strong/High
Consider prophylactic TAE in selected high risk cases (hemodynamic instability, posterior duodenal ulcer, >2cm size, uncertain durable hemostasis) Conditional/Very Low
Recurrent bleeding: repeat endoscopy with OTS clip considered Strong/Moderate
Second attempt failed: TAE; surgery if unavailable/unsuccessful Strong/Strong
Resume anticoagulation based on thromboembolic risk ASAP Strong/Low
Test for H. pylori at index endoscopy; treat if positive Strong/High
If H. pylori negative: repeat testing within 4 weeks to confirm true negative Strong/High
Document successful H. pylori eradication Strong/High
Initiate iron therapy before discharge for iron deficiency/anemia Conditional/Low
Early oral nutrition within 24h after endoscopic hemostasis if durable hemostasis achieved Conditional/Low
6. Future Research Directions
6.1 Pre-Endoscopy Management
  • Optimal hemodynamic resuscitation strategies
  • Transfusion thresholds in specific populations
  • Airway protection indications and timing
  • PPI optimization protocols
  • Prokinetic medication efficacy
  • Impact on mortality, surgery, repeat endoscopy, hospital stay
  • Machine learning/AI for risk prediction vs. traditional scoring (GBS, Rockall, AIMS65)
  • External validation and prospective impact studies needed
6.2 Endoscopic Management
  • Timing of endoscopy in hemodynamically unstable patients despite resuscitation
  • Role of Doppler endoscopic probe in guiding hemostasis therapy
  • Defining ulcer characteristics (location, size, Forrest class) that optimize OTS clip use
  • Learning curves for OTS clip competence acquisition
  • RCTs of topical hemostatic agents vs. standard endoscopic modalities
  • Evaluation of emerging therapies (tissue suturing, tissue adhesives, hemostatic forceps)
6.3 Postendoscopy Management
  • Acid suppressive efficacy of P-CABs after endoscopic hemostasis
  • Impact of blood in upper GI tract and PPI use on H. pylori testing during acute bleeding
  • Standardization of endoscopy reports in peptic ulcer bleeding
  • Establishing/evaluating key performance indicators for peptic ulcer bleeding management
7. Abbreviations
  • CI: Confidence Interval
  • CV: Cardiovascular
  • DEP: Doppler Endoscopic Probe
  • DOAC: Direct Oral Anticoagulant
  • GBS: Glasgow-Blatchford Score
  • Hb: Hemoglobin
  • IV: Intravenous
  • OR: Odds Ratio
  • OTS: Over-The-Scope
  • P-CAB: Potassium-Competitive Acid Blocker
  • PPI: Proton Pump Inhibitor
  • RBC: Red Blood Cell
  • RCT: Randomized Controlled Trial
  • RR: Risk Ratio
  • TAE: Transcatheter Angiographic Embolization
  • TTS: Through-The-Scope
  • UGIH: Upper Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage
  • VKA: Vitamin K Antagonist
